Chapter B: Surfaces

B.1 Quadratic Surfaces

The most general quadratic surface (also called a quadric surface) in is given by

where are constants, not all of equal to zero.

This can be written in matrix form as

The mixed terms , , and correspond to rotations of the coordinate axes. By translating and rotating coordinates, every nondegenerate quadric can be reduced to one of the following standard forms.

B.2 Ellipsoid

B.3 Hyperboloid of One Sheet

B.4 Hyperboloid of Two Sheets

B.5 Elliptic Cone

B.6 Elliptic Paraboloid

B.7 Hyperbolic Paraboloid

B.8 Elliptic Cylinder

B.9 Hyperbolic Cylinder

B.10 Parabolic Cylinder

Remark.

Every quadratic surface is the zero set of a polynomial of degree two in the variables , , and .
